cells.title: Overview
cells.source: This notebook implements **OPENSIMPLEST**, the smallest stock-flow consistent (SFC) open-economy model from Zezza (Levy Economics Institute Working Paper No. 1105, January 2026). The runnable model uses paper-style superscripted variable names — `H^P`, `B^S`, `B^{CB}`, `yd^{HS}`, and others — with `pow(base, exponent)` for exponentiation so `^` remains available in names.

cells.title: Balance sheet matrix
cells.description: Compact balance-sheet view for the OPENSIMPLEST open-economy model.
cells.more:
This is Table 1 in Zezza (2026, section 3.2). The model abstracts from physical capital, inventories, loans, and equities: **all wealth is financial**. The private sector holds high-powered money (`H^P`), domestic government bills (`B^P`), and foreign bills (`BF^P`, valued in domestic currency at `xr`). Government bills are liabilities of the government and assets of the private sector, central bank, and RoW. Foreign bills are RoW liabilities and private-sector assets.

Rows sum to zero horizontally (every asset has a matching liability). Column sums including net worth are also zero. The RoW net position is the mirror of domestic **net foreign assets** (`NFA`), so valuation effects on `BF^P` when `xr` moves are recorded symmetrically in private wealth and the external position.

cells.title: Transactions-flow matrix
cells.description: Transactions-flow matrix for the OPENSIMPLEST open-economy model.
cells.more:
Table 2 (section 3.3) records all monetary flows between sectors. The production column closes to GDP (`y`). The private sector receives wage income (embedded in `y`), bill interest, valuation gains on foreign assets, pays taxes and spending (`pe`), and records **net acquisition of financial assets** (`NAFA`). The government spends (`g`), collects taxes (`t`), pays interest on debt, and receives central-bank profits (`F^{CB}`). The RoW trades (`x`, `im`), pays and receives cross-border interest, and books valuation effects.

The **sectoral balances** row encodes the fundamental identity from national accounts:

`NAFA - PSBR - CA = 0`

(private net lending minus public net borrowing minus the current account). The model checks this each period via `sectoral_check`. Flow-of-funds rows link stock changes (`d(H^P)`, `d(B^P)`, and so on) to the sectoral balance identities.

cells.title: Transaction flow sequence
cells.description: Canvas-rendered sequence view generated from the transactions-flow matrix at the selected simulation period.
cells.note: Use Reset and Next step to manually reveal flows in order.
cells.more: Step through the transaction matrix period by period to see how each flow connects sectors. This is the same accounting structure Zezza uses to motivate the behavioral blocks: demand (`pe`, `g`, `x`, `im`), interest and valuation flows, sectoral balances, and stock accumulation.

cells.title: Equation dependency graph
cells.description: Dependency view of the equations organized by sector strips and accounting bands.
cells.note: Compare with Figure 1A in Zezza (2026) — a compact graph relative to OPENFLEX and OPENSIME.
cells.more: Zezza contrasts this dependency structure with OPENFLEX and OPENSIME (Figures 1B–1C in the paper). OPENSIMPLEST drops the price block, exchange-rate expectations in portfolio demand, and the explicit foreign-country equation system, but preserves the dynamic core: income → trade → current account → exchange rate → portfolio → government and central bank.

cells.title: OPENSIMPLEST model

equations.name: yd^R
equations.expression: y + (lag(r) * lag(B^P) + lag(r^F) * lag(BF^P) * xr) - t

equations.name: t
equations.expression: theta * (y + lag(r) * lag(B^P) + lag(r^F) * lag(BF^P) * xr)

equations.name: NAFA
equations.desc: Net acquisition of financial assets by domestic private sector (NAFA).

equations.name: CA
equations.expression: TB + lag(r^F) * lag(BF^P) * xr - lag(r) * lag(B^W)

equations.name: NFA
equations.desc: Net foreign assets (country external position, NETFA).

equations.name: B^W
equations.desc: Foreign holdings of domestic government bills (residual from NETFA identity).

equations.name: BF^P
equations.desc: Foreign bills held by domestic households (in foreign currency).
equations.expression: v * (lambda20 - lambda21 * r + lambda22 * r^F) / xr

equations.name: B^{CB}
equations.desc: Domestic bills held by the central bank.

equations.name: B^S
equations.desc: Total stock of domestic government bills.

equations.name: xr
equations.desc: Nominal exchange rate (domestic currency per unit of foreign currency).

equations.name: sectoral_check
equations.desc: Sectoral balance identity (NAFA - PSBR - CA, should equal zero).

cells.more:
Equations follow Zezza (2026, section 4), aligned with Godley & Lavoie (2007) notation.

**Income and wealth (4.1).** Regular disposable income `yd^R` includes labour income (`y`), domestic and foreign interest, and taxes. Haig–Simons income `yd^{HS}` adds valuation effects `d(xr) * lag(BF^P)`. Wealth `v` accumulates saving out of `yd^{HS} - pe`. Consumption `pe = alpha1 * yd^{HS} + alpha2 * lag(v)` is the standard SFC demand function.

**External sector (4.2).** Log-linear trade functions `x` and `im` use **lagged** `xr` for J-curve dynamics. The current account `CA` adds the trade balance and net cross-border interest. Net foreign assets `NFA` integrate `CA` plus valuation effects. Foreign holdings of domestic bills `B^W` are residual from the balance sheet.

**Portfolio (4.3).** Domestic and foreign bill demands are Tobinesque functions of `v`, `r`, and `r^F`. Money `H^P` is the residual asset closing the private column — vertical consistency. Horizontal consistency requires `lambda12 = lambda21`.

**Government and central bank (4.4).** The central bank is the residual purchaser of bills (`B^{CB} = B^S - B^P - B^W`), sets `r`, and transfers profits `F^{CB}` to government. Money supply `H^S` equals CB bill holdings. Public debt `B^S` accumulates the PSBR.

**Exchange rate (4.5).** `xr = I(phi * (-lag(CA)))` — depreciation pressure when the lagged current account is in deficit. This is the main closure difference from OPENFLEX (asset-market clearing) and OPENSIME (contemporaneous BOP clearing).

cells.title: Solver
cells.more: The model is solved period by period with Newton's method. Lagged stocks enter portfolio demands, trade functions, interest flows, and the exchange-rate rule. The `sectoral_check` equation should remain near zero each period, confirming `NAFA - PSBR - CA = 0`.

cells.title: Externals

externals.name: g
externals.desc: Government expenditure on goods and services.

externals.name: r
externals.desc: Interest rate on domestic government bills.

externals.name: r^F
externals.desc: Interest rate on foreign bills (exogenous).

externals.name: y^F
externals.desc: Foreign income (exogenous).

externals.name: alpha1
externals.desc: Propensity to consume out of disposable income.

externals.name: alpha2
externals.desc: Propensity to consume out of wealth.

externals.name: theta
externals.desc: Average tax rate on income.

externals.name: eps0
externals.desc: Export equation constant term.

externals.name: eps1
externals.desc: Export elasticity with respect to the exchange rate.

externals.name: eps2
externals.desc: Export elasticity with respect to foreign income.

externals.name: mu0
externals.desc: Import equation constant term.

externals.name: mu1
externals.desc: Import elasticity with respect to the exchange rate.

externals.name: mu2
externals.desc: Import elasticity with respect to domestic income.

externals.name: lambda10
externals.desc: Portfolio share parameter: domestic bills (baseline).

externals.name: lambda11
externals.desc: Portfolio sensitivity to domestic interest rate (domestic bills).

externals.name: lambda12
externals.desc: Portfolio sensitivity to foreign interest rate (domestic bills).

externals.name: lambda20
externals.desc: Portfolio share parameter: foreign bills (baseline).

externals.name: lambda21
externals.desc: Portfolio sensitivity to domestic interest rate (foreign bills).

externals.name: lambda22
externals.desc: Portfolio sensitivity to foreign interest rate (foreign bills).

externals.name: phi
externals.desc: Exchange-rate adjustment speed.

cells.title: Initial values
cells.more: Initial stocks and flows reproduce the paper's balanced starting position: trade and current account near zero, sectoral balances aligned, and portfolio stocks consistent with the balance sheet. The baseline run then integrates forward 150 periods toward a new stationary configuration.

cells.title: Baseline
cells.description: Baseline simulation for the Levy WP 1105 aligned OPENSIMPLEST model over 150 periods.
cells.more: The baseline integrates 150 periods with fixed parameters. Output `y` rises from the initial `80` toward roughly `97.5` by period 150 as portfolios and the exchange rate adjust. The current account and sectoral balances converge toward zero, consistent with a stationary external position. All scenario runs below branch from this baseline at period 5.

cells.title: Baseline external sector
cells.more: Zezza's baseline panels track the external constraint: the trade balance `TB`, current account `CA`, exchange rate `xr`, and output `y`. In the baseline, `CA` and `TB` settle near zero while `y` and `xr` drift to new steady levels as stocks adjust.

cells.title: Baseline sectoral balances
cells.more: The sectoral-balances identity `NAFA - PSBR - CA = 0` is visible when the three series sum visually to zero. In the baseline all three converge toward zero as the model approaches stationarity.

cells.title: Baseline summary (periods 1, 75, 150)
cells.more: Compare early, mid, and late periods. By period 150 the baseline reaches `y` near `97.5`, `xr` near `1.19`, and near-zero `CA` and sectoral balances — the stationary configuration from which scenario shocks depart at period 5.

cells.title: Section 5 — Simulations
cells.source: Zezza replicates four shocks from Godley & Lavoie (2007, ch. 12) and Carnevali (2021). Each experiment applies permanently from period 5. Charts plot scenario paths against the baseline reference trace.
cells.more:
Section 5 shows that OPENSIMPLEST reproduces the **qualitative** medium-run responses of larger open-economy SFC models for fiscal, external-demand, and portfolio shocks. Differences appear mainly for **pure monetary shocks** in the very short run, because the lagged current-account exchange-rate closure does not deliver the immediate depreciation seen under asset-market clearing in OPENFLEX.

| Scenario | Shock (from period 5) | Paper figure |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 5.1 Export competitiveness | `eps0`: `-2.1` → `-2.2` | Figure 2 |
| 5.2 Fiscal expansion | `g`: `16` → `18` | Figure 3 |
| 5.3 Lower domestic rate | `r`: `0.03` → `0.029` | Figure 4 |
| 5.4 Liquidity preference | `lambda10`: `0.7` → `0.65`, `lambda20`: `0.25` → `0.3` | Figure 5 |

cells.title: Scenario 5.1 — Step-fall in exports
cells.source: The export intercept `eps0` falls from `-2.1` to `-2.2` from period 5, reducing international competitiveness. Exports drop on impact; the current account weakens; the pound depreciates (`xr` rises); and activity eventually rebounds to a higher steady state as trade responds.
cells.more: Zezza describes this as a step in the "residual indicator" of British competitiveness (section 5.1). Qualitatively: GDP falls initially as exports and the current account deteriorate, then **rebounds** after depreciation improves the trade balance. The government deficit (`PSBR`) rises while private `NAFA` is squeezed; as the current account recovers, private asset accumulation resumes.

cells.title: Scenario 5.1 — Export shock
cells.more: On impact at period 5, `x` falls relative to baseline (from about `26` toward `24`). `xr` depreciates (rises to about `1.20` vs `1.15` in the baseline at the same date). By period 150, `y` exceeds the baseline (`~99` vs `~97.5`) and `xr` remains higher (`~1.30` vs `~1.19`), matching the paper's higher steady state after competitiveness loss plus depreciation.

cells.title: Scenario 5.1 — External sector
cells.more: Compare to Figure 2 panels A–C. The trade and current accounts weaken first; `xr` depreciates; `y` dips then climbs above the baseline reference in the medium run.

cells.title: Scenario 5.1 — Sectoral balances
cells.more: Panel D in the paper: `PSBR` rises and `NAFA` is compressed when the current account turns negative; both rebalance as external adjustment proceeds.

cells.title: Scenario 5.2 — Expansionary fiscal policy
cells.source: Government spending `g` rises permanently from `16` to `18` (a two-unit increase) from period 5. Fiscal stimulus boosts GDP; the current account deteriorates; the exchange rate depreciates rather than appreciates — contradicting the Mundell–Fleming offset story.
cells.more: Zezza (section 5.2) highlights this as a Keynesian open-economy result already present in OPENFLEX: higher `g` raises demand, weakens the current account, and induces **depreciation** that reinforces the stimulus by slowing imports and supporting exports. Mainstream models often predict appreciation under flexible rates; the SFC closure delivers the opposite qualitative pattern.

cells.title: Scenario 5.2 — Fiscal expansion
cells.more: Permanent `g = 18` lifts `y` above the baseline throughout the adjustment path. `CA` turns more negative as imports rise with income; `xr` depreciates to partially offset the external drag — the fiscal multiplier is reinforced, not crowded out by appreciation.

cells.title: Scenario 5.2 — Output, external sector, and exchange rate
cells.more: Figure 3 in the paper. Watch `y` step up with `g`, `CA` weaken, and `xr` depreciate — the joint fiscal–external adjustment characteristic of Godley–Lavoie open-economy models.

cells.title: Scenario 5.3 — Lower domestic interest rate
cells.source: The central bank cuts `r` from `0.03` to `0.029` (a 0.1 percentage-point reduction) from period 5. Zezza highlights this case as the experiment where the lagged current-account exchange-rate closure matters most relative to OPENFLEX.
cells.more:
Section 5.3 is the monetary experiment where closures diverge. In OPENSIMPLEST the exchange rate does not jump to clear asset markets; it responds to **lagged** current-account positions through `xr = I(phi * (-lag(CA)))`.

The paper's mechanism: a lower `r` reduces interest paid to the RoW on `B^W`, improving the current account on the income account; with the Harrodian rule, that flow imbalance gradually pulls `xr` toward appreciation (a lower domestic-per-foreign rate, or higher `1/xr` in the paper's sterling panels). The medium-run outcome is **contractionary** — tighter net exports and weaker private balance sheets — unlike the immediate depreciation often seen in two-country portfolio-clearing models.

Use the chart below to compare this scenario with the baseline reference trace and with Figure 4 in the paper.

cells.title: Scenario 5.3 — Interest rate cut
cells.more: Figure 4 in the paper. Track `CA`, `xr`, and `y` against the baseline. The paper emphasizes a contractionary medium-run adjustment once the external account and trade flows rebalance after the rate cut.

cells.title: Scenario 5.3 — External adjustment after rate cut
cells.more: Figure 4. Contrast this adjustment path with the depreciation episodes in scenarios 5.1 and 5.2. Private wealth `v` and output `y` move with the trade channel as the external account rebalances.

cells.title: Scenario 5.4 — Liquidity preference / portfolio shift
cells.source: From period 5, households shift portfolio preferences toward foreign bills (`lambda20` up to `0.3`) and away from domestic bills (`lambda10` down to `0.65`). Money demand remains residual. The shock mimics greater appetite for foreign assets in G&L's liquidity-preference experiment.
cells.more:
Zezza implements a **specular** change in `lambda10` and `lambda20` so the shock is a substitution between interest-bearing assets, not a flight from money (section 5.4). In one-country form only domestic portfolio parameters move; the RoW counterpart is absorbed through `B^W` and `B^{CB}` residuals.

Dynamics are two-phase: initial **depreciation** as wealth shifts toward foreign bills, then medium-run **appreciation** as current-account and valuation effects rebalance the external position — the pattern emphasized in OPENFLEX and OPENSIME.

cells.title: Scenario 5.4 — Portfolio shift
cells.more: Dual shocks in one `rangeInclusive` block mirror the EViews code (`lambda10 = 0.65`, `lambda20 = 0.3` from period 5). Portfolio reallocation moves `BF^P` and `B^P` in opposite directions while `H^P` clears residually.

cells.title: Scenario 5.4 — Exchange rate and external sector
cells.more: Figure 5. Look for depreciation early in the shock window, then gradual appreciation as `CA` and valuation effects on `BF^P` feed back through `yd^{HS}` and trade.
